revision.comment: Bot: mark 1 deleted QID(s) as DELETED_QID in ill templates [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24432061637|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ja:下呂温泉]][[zh:下呂溫泉]] {{infobox Onsen | Name = Gero Onsen (下呂温泉) | image = Gero-Onsen001.JPG | Comment = Hot Spring Town {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=300|coord={{coord|35|48|29|N|137|14|21|E}}}} | Location = {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|qid=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|Gero, Gifu|en|Gero, Gifu|qid=Q819639|lt=Gero City|1=Gero, Gifu}} Mori | Latitude Degrees = 35 | Latitude Minutes = 48 | Latitude Seconds = 29 | N (Northern Latitude/Southern Latitude) = N | Longitude Degrees = 137 | Longitude Minutes = 14 | Longitude Seconds = 21 | E (Eastern Longitude/Western Longitude) = E | Access = Rail: {{ill|Takayama Main Line|en|Takayama Main Line|qid=Q1188357|lt=Takayama Main Line|1=Takayama Main Line}} immediately from {{ill|Gero Station|en|Gero Station|qid=Q873111|lt=Gero Station|1=Gero Station}}
Car: Use {{ill|Japan National Route 41|en|Japan National Route 41|qid=Q6157478|lt=National Route 41|1=Japan National Route 41}}, {{ill|Japan National Route 257|en|Japan National Route 257|qid=Q6157057|lt=National Route 257|1=Japan National Route 257}}, {{ill|Nōtobi Expressway|ja|濃飛横断自動車道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nōtobi Expressway|1=Nohi crossing motorway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11566126}}, etc.
City-operated parking available near the hot spring town. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = 9 or above | Classification of Liquid Nature = Alkaline | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | External Links = http://www.gero-spa.or.jp/ | Special Notes = }} '''Gero Onsen''' (下呂温泉) is a {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|qid=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}} located in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|qid=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|Gero, Gifu|en|Gero, Gifu|qid=Q819639|lt=Gero City|1=Gero, Gifu}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hida Province|en|Hida Province|qid=Q1056535|lt=Hida Province|1=Hida Province}} and, within Gifu Prefecture, the former {{ill|Mashita District, Gifu|en|Mashita District, Gifu|qid=Q6783168|lt=Masuda District|1=Mashita District, Gifu}} {{ill|Gero Town|ja|下呂町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Town|1=Gero|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11360704}}).[https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/2033/ Gero Onsen | Gifu Attractions | Travel Japan | JNTO] During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Banri Shūkyū|ja|万里集九|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banri Shūkyū|1=Banrishūku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11353943}} counted it together with {{ill|Arima Onsen|en|Arima Onsen|qid=Q4790673|lt=Arima Onsen|1=Arima Onsen}} and {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|qid=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen|1=Kusatsu Onsen}} as the "Three Famous Hot Springs," and in the Edo period, {{ill|Hayashi Razan|en|Hayashi Razan|qid=Q558279|lt=Hayashi Razan|1=Hayashi Razan}}[https://allabout-japan.com/en/article/183/ The 3 Most Famous Hot Springs in Japan | All About Japan] also described these as "Japan's Three Famous Hot Springs" ('''Japan's Three Famous Hot Springs'''){{Cite web|url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/ecotourism/try-ecotourism/certification/gero/kousou/images/document/kousou.pdf|title={{ill|Gero City Ecotourism Promotion Comprehensive Plan|ja|下呂市エコツーリズム推進全体構想（下呂市エコツーリズム推進協議会）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero City Ecotourism Promotion Comprehensive Plan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|accessdate=2021-12-01|website={{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|qid=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment|1=Ministry of the Environment (Japan)}}}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.gero-spa.com/access/doc/P06-P07.pdf|title={{ill|Gero Onsen Town Map|ja|下呂温泉街マップ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen Town Map|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|accessdate=2021-12-01|website={{ill|Gero Onsen Tourism Association|ja|下呂温泉観光協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen Tourism Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}}} (Note: At that time the hot spring was referred to as “Yunoshima” rather than “Gero”). == Spring Quality == * Alkaline {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, source temperature 84°C == Hot Spring Town == [[File:Gero-onsen01s3200.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Hot spring town and {{ill|Nakane Mountain|ja|中根山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakane Mountain|1=Mount Nakane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11366225}} (Gero Fuji)]] Lodging facilities such as {{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|qid=Q562930|lt=ryokan|1=Ryokan}} and {{ill|Hotel|en|Hotel|qid=Q27686|lt=hotels|1=Hotel}} line the banks along the {{ill|Hida River|en|Hida River|qid=Q3366160|lt=Hida River|1=Hida River}} (also known as the {{ill|Masuda River|ja|益田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masuda River|1=Masuda River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11581254}}), centered around {{ill|Gero Station|en|Gero Station|qid=Q873111|lt=Gero Station|1=Gero Station}}. In addition to along the river, accommodations are found on the slopes of the hills on both banks – many of which offer shuttle bus service to {{ill|Gero Station|en|Gero Station|qid=Q873111|lt=Gero Station|1=Gero Station}}.例：{{Cite web|url=http://www.yunoshimakan.co.jp/access/|title=Yunoshimakan – Transportation and Sightseeing|publisher={{ill|Yunoshimakan|ja|湯之島館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoshimakan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=2018-09-28|accessdate=2019-10-13|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20180928111323/http://www.yunoshimakan.co.jp/access/|archivedate=2018-09-28}} Note: In the town names of {{ill|Gero, Gifu|en|Gero, Gifu|qid=Q819639|lt=Gero City|1=Gero, Gifu}}, the western side of the river is known as {{ill|Koda|en|Koda|qid=Q1223964|lt=Kōden|1=Koda}} (which includes {{ill|Gero Station|en|Gero Station|qid=Q873111|lt=Gero Station|1=Gero Station}}), while on the eastern side the area north of the road beyond {{ill|Gero Ōhashi|ja|下呂大橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Ōhashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (along Adano-dōri) is called {{ill|Yunoshima|ja|湯之島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoshima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the southern side is known as Mori. Most of the accommodations fall within these three districts, although the far northwestern lodging is located in the former {{ill|Hagiwara Town|ja|萩原町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara Town|1=Hagiwara-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11620353}} area of {{ill|Mashita District, Gifu|en|Mashita District, Gifu|qid=Q6783168|lt=Masuda District|1=Mashita District, Gifu}} (currently {{ill|Hagiwara-cho, Gero City|ja|下呂市萩原町西上田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara-cho, Gero City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). == Hotels and Ryokan == There is a wide range of lodging available – from traditional ryokan and luxury hotels to business hotels. For details, please refer to the website of the [http://www.gero-spa.or.jp/]. Note that some accommodations are not affiliated with the cooperative (for example, {{ill|Ōedo Onsen Monogatari Gero Shinkan|ja|大江戸温泉物語 下呂新館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōedo Onsen Monogatari Gero Shinkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). The Gero Onsen Ryokan Cooperative also issues a "Yumeguri Tegata" (Hot Spring Tour Voucher). Although the vouchers have an expiration date, each voucher allows the holder to visit and bathe at any three participating establishments.{{Cite web|url=http://www.gero-spa.or.jp/yu_meguri/|title=Yumeguri Tegata|publisher={{ill|Gero Onsen Ryokan Cooperative|ja|下呂温泉旅館協同組合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen Ryokan Cooperative|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135493039}}|date=2019-08-14|accessdate=2019-10-13|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20190814104626/http://www.gero-spa.or.jp/yu_meguri/|archivedate=2019-08-14}} == Public Baths == There are three public baths ({{ill|soto-yu|ja|外湯|1=Sotoyu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/soto-yu|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} ({{ill|Public bathing|en|Public bathing|qid=Q785952|lt=Public Bathhouse|1=Public bathing}})): Kua Garden Open-Air Bath, {{ill|Shirasagi no Yu|ja|白鷺の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirasagi no Yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kōno Yu|ja|幸乃湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno Yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.gero.lg.jp/kankou/node_7779/node_29737/node_37101/node_37104|title=Day-Use Bath Facilities in the Gero Onsen Town|publisher={{ill|Gero City Tourism Division|ja|下呂市役所 観光商工部 観光課|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero City Tourism Division|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=2019-03-20|accessdate=2019-10-13|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20190402011550/http://www.city.gero.lg.jp/kankou/node_7779/node_29737/node_37101/node_37104|archivedate=2019-04-02}} == Climate == The climate of Gero Onsen is a mountain inland climate, being surrounded by mountains. There is a significant temperature variation throughout the day, so in spring and autumn a jacket that can be easily put on or taken off is recommended.[https://www.hankyu-travel.com/onsen/gero/hotspring.php]{{ill|Gero Onsen Tidbits|ja|下呂温泉豆知識|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen Tidbits|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Hankyu Travel, viewed February 26, 2020) In the Hida region, particularly in the southern parts and on the Pacific side of the water system, snowfall occurs but is not excessive.[https://ja.weatherspark.com/y/143604/下呂市、日本における年間の平均的な気候　下呂市 Weatherspark, viewed February 26, 2020] == Access == === Rail === * {{ill|Central Japan Railway Company|en|Central Japan Railway Company|qid=Q513679|lt=Central Japan Railway Company|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} ([[:en:JR Central|JR Central]]) {{ill|Takayama Main Line|en|Takayama Main Line|qid=Q1188357|lt=Takayama Main Line|1=Takayama Main Line}}: {{ill|Nagoya Station|en|Nagoya Station|qid=Q49381|lt=Nagoya Station|1=Nagoya Station}} – {{ill|Gifu Station|en|Gifu Station|qid=Q1144885|lt=Gifu Station|1=Gifu Station}} – {{ill|Mino-Ōta Station|en|Mino-Ōta Station|qid=Q872979|lt=Minota Station|1=Mino-Ōta Station}} – '''{{ill|Gero Station|en|Gero Station|qid=Q873111|lt=Gero Station|1=Gero Station}}''' – {{ill|Takayama Station|en|Takayama Station|qid=Q874020|lt=Takayama Station|1=Takayama Station}} – {{ill|Hida-Furukawa Station|en|Hida-Furukawa Station|qid=Q875450|lt=Hidakagawa Station|1=Hida-Furukawa Station}} – {{ill|Inotani Station|en|Inotani Station|qid=Q5357888|lt=Inotani Station|1=Inotani Station}} – {{ill|Etchū-Yatsuo Station|en|Etchū-Yatsuo Station|qid=Q5402530|lt=Echizen-Hachioji Station|1=Etchū-Yatsuo Station}} – {{ill|Toyama Station|en|Toyama Station|qid=Q1053070|lt=Toyama Station|1=Toyama Station}} (when taking the fastest {{ill|Limited Express|ja|特急|lt=Limited Express|lt_ja=特急|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Limited Express|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 特別急行列車|1=Limited express|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6549533}} train, the {{ill|Hida|ja|ひだ号|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hida|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) ** Limited Express Hida also operates one round-trip from {{ill|Ōsaka Station|en|Ōsaka Station|qid=Q801694|lt=Osaka Station|1=Ōsaka Station}}. === Bus === * ＜Route Bus＞ {{ill|Takayama Nōtobi Bus Center|ja|高山濃飛バスセンター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayama Nōtobi Bus Center|1=Takayama Nohi Bus Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11669403}} – Gero Onsen Shita – Gero Bus Center (operated by {{ill|Nōtobi Bus|ja|濃飛乗合自動車|lt=Nōtobi Bus|lt_ja=濃飛バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nōtobi Bus|1=Nohi Noriai Jidosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11566124}}) ** At the Takayama Nōtobi Bus Center, connecting highway buses run to Shinjuku, Nagoya, Gifu, Kyoto/Osaka, as well as limited express buses to Kamikōchi, Matsumoto, Shirakawa-go, and Kanazawa. * ＜Limited Express Bus＞ Gero Bus Center – Gero Onsen – Takayama Nōtobi Bus Center – Shirakawa-go (operated by {{ill|Nōtobi Bus|ja|濃飛乗合自動車|lt=Nōtobi Bus|lt_ja=濃飛バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nōtobi Bus|1=Nohi Noriai Jidosha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11566124}}) * When staying at accommodations affiliated with the {{ill|Gero Onsen Ryokan Cooperative|ja|下呂温泉旅館協同組合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen Ryokan Cooperative|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135493039}}, a direct bus from {{ill|Nagoya Station|en|Nagoya Station|qid=Q49381|lt=Nagoya Station|1=Nagoya Station}} is available (reservation required).{{Cite web|url=http://www.gero-spa.or.jp/bas/|title=Direct Bus for Gero Onsen|publisher={{ill|Gero Onsen Ryokan Cooperative|ja|下呂温泉旅館協同組合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen Ryokan Cooperative|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135493039}}|date=2019-04-12|accessdate=2019-10-13|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20190401232636/http://www.gero-spa.or.jp/bas/|archivedate=2019-04-12}} === By Car === * {{Ja Exp Route Sign|E19}} {{ill|Chūō Expressway|en|Chūō Expressway|qid=Q1091276|lt=Chūō Expressway|1=Chūō Expressway}} from {{ill|Nakatsugawa Interchange|ja|中津川インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsugawa Interchange|1=Nakatsugawa interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10875793}} via {{ill|Japan National Route 257|en|Japan National Route 257|qid=Q6157057|lt=National Route 257|1=Japan National Route 257}}. * {{Ja Exp Route Sign|E41}} {{ill|Tōkai-Hokuriku Expressway|en|Tōkai-Hokuriku Expressway|qid=Q7862674|lt=Tōkai-Hokuriku Expressway|1=Tōkai-Hokuriku Expressway}} from {{ill|Gujo-Hachiman Interchange|ja|郡上八幡インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gujo-Hachiman Interchange|1=Gujo-hachiman interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11643076}} via {{ill|Japan National Route 256|en|Japan National Route 256|qid=Q6157056|lt=National Route 256|1=Japan National Route 256}} and {{ill|Japan National Route 41|en|Japan National Route 41|qid=Q6157478|lt=National Route 41|1=Japan National Route 41}}. == History == The origin of the hot spring dates back to between the Enki and Tenryaku periods of the 10th century, when a hot spring began erupting near the summit of Yukagamamine (1,067 m) located to the east of the current hot spring area. It was reputed to have healing properties, attracting therapeutic bathers from the very beginning.[{{NDLDC|1057310/14}}　下呂温泉]『湯の街下呂』 (Gero Town, 1933) In 1265 (Bun'ei 2), the eruption from the mountain summit ceased, but a new spring was discovered on the riverbed of the {{ill|Hida River|en|Hida River|qid=Q3366160|lt=Hida River|1=Hida River}}, which is the present hot spring area. This discovery is preserved in the {{ill|Hot Spring Discovery Legend|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Discovery Legend|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Origin Myth|qq=}}, specifically the "White Egret Legend" in which {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} transformed into a white egret to signal the location of the spring. With the shifting of the spring’s outlet, the statue of Yakushi Nyorai that had been enshrined at Yukagamamine was relocated to the village, establishing the {{ill|Onsen-dera|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen-dera|1=Onsen Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11562939}}. During the Muromachi period, the {{ill|Five Mountains Monks|ja|五山僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Mountains Monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都五山|1=Kyoto Gozan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8193432}} and {{ill|Banri Shūkyū|ja|万里集九|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banri Shūkyū|1=Banrishūku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11353943}} introduced Gero Onsen nationwide as one of the "Three Famous Hot Springs" along with {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|qid=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen|1=Kusatsu Onsen}} and {{ill|Arima Onsen|en|Arima Onsen|qid=Q4790673|lt=Arima Onsen|1=Arima Onsen}}[http://www.onsenji.jp/history/index.html?admin=view　History of Onsen-dera] Gero Onsen, Iorui Reizan, Onsen-dera. In 1621, {{ill|Hayashi Razan|en|Hayashi Razan|qid=Q558279|lt=Hayashirasan|1=Hayashi Razan}} also cited it as a famed hot spring in his writings. The Onsen-dera, built at the foot of {{ill|Nakane Mountain|ja|中根山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakane Mountain|1=Mount Nakane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11366225}}, is said to have been founded in the Edo period (1671). At that time the spring was called “Yushima,” and later, Yunoshima Onsen became used as an alternate name for Gero Onsen.. Although the hot spring area has repeatedly suffered catastrophic damage from flooding of the {{ill|Hida River|en|Hida River|qid=Q3366160|lt=Hida River|1=Hida River}}, it was always rebuilt; however, during the great flood of the {{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|qid=Q1327259|lt=Ansei|1=Ansei}} era, the hot spring vein was destroyed and the outlet lost, leading to a period of decline in the Meiji era. In an effort to revive the spring, local residents began boring excavation during the {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q329174}}, and in the early Shōwa period, anticipating the opening of the {{ill|Takayama Main Line|en|Takayama Main Line|qid=Q1188357|lt=Takayama Main Line|1=Takayama Main Line}} at {{ill|Gero Station|en|Gero Station|qid=Q873111|lt=Gero Station|1=Gero Station}} in 1930 (Shōwa 5), Nagoya businessman Takehachi Iwata – the founder of {{ill|Madras|ja|マドラス (企業)|lt=Madras|lt_ja=マドラス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Madras|1=Madras|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11340682}} – embarked on a mining venture and opened Yunoshima-kan in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.niwa-ae.com/ourworks/old_yunoshima.htm Yunoshima-kan] (Niwa Eiji Architectural Office) The building of this ryokan has since been designated as a Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan.[http://www.yunoshimakan.co.jp/about/history.html History of Yunoshima-kan] (Yunoshima-kan, viewed February 20, 2020). == Origin of the Place Name == The name “Gero” dates back to the Ritsuryō system. According to the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|qid=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}, in 776 AD (Hōki 2), near Gero Onsen there existed stations – one, {{ill|Sugata Station|ja|菅田駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugata Station|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|qid=Q864156|lt=Mino Province|1=Mino Province}}, and another, Ban'yū (Uwedome Station), in {{ill|Hida Province|en|Hida Province|qid=Q1056535|lt=Hida Province|1=Hida Province}} ({{ill|Ōno District, Gifu|en|Ōno District, Gifu|qid=Q31671|lt=Ōno District|1=Ōno District, Gifu}}).[http://www.city.gero.lg.jp/kankou/view.rbz?of=1&ik=0&pnp=14&cd=26 Gero City Tourism Information] However, because the two stations were far apart and the roads were steep, a new station, Shimonodome Station, was established midway. Over time, the pronunciation evolved from “Shimonodome” to “Gero.” Sugata Station was located in what is now {{ill|Sugata, Kanayama-cho, Gero City|ja|下呂市金山町菅田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugata, Kanayama-cho, Gero City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Ban'yū Station was in {{ill|Hagiwara-cho, Gero City|ja|下呂市萩原町上呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara-cho, Gero City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. == Landmarks == === Fountain Pool === [[File:Gero Spa Kawararoten.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Hot Spring Fountain Area (Open-Air Bath on the Riverbed)]] At Gero Onsen, a hot spring facility known as the "Fountain Pool" was established in 1983 (Shōwa 58) along the riverbed of the {{ill|Hida River|en|Hida River|qid=Q3366160|lt=Hida River|1=Hida River}} so that visitors can bathe casually.{{Cite web|url=https://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/20211128-OYT1T50159/|title=Customers Not Observing Bathing Etiquette Cause Trouble, Leading to Complaints – Famous Hot Spring Now “Foot Bath Only”|accessdate=2021-12-01|website={{ill|Yomiuri Shimbun|en|Yomiuri Shimbun|qid=Q645218|lt=Yomiuri Shimbun|1=The Yomiuri Shimbun}}}} Although the Fountain Pool, as a free open-air bath, has repeatedly been submerged by heavy rains and received sediment deposits, it has continued in operation with adjustments to its location and scale. From February 1, 2010 (Heisei 22), both men and women were required to wear swimsuits when using it[http://www.gero-spa.com/spot/detail/2 Guidelines on Swimsuit Use at the Fountain Pool – Gero Onsen Tourism Association][http://www.city.gero.lg.jp/kankou/view.rbz?nd=101&ik=1&pnp=100&pnp=101&cd=26 Information on the Fountain Pool – Gero City Tourism Division] However, due to complaints over rule violations and concerns for the surrounding scenery, its operation has been revised. From December 1, 2021 (Reiwa 3), the Fountain Pool has been designated exclusively as a foot bath.{{Cite news|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/DA3S15128546.html|title=Gero Onsen Symbol Now Dedicated to Foot Baths – Riverbed, Without Enforcing Swimsuit Rules: Asahi Shimbun Digital|newspaper=|date=2021-11-30}} * Location (Hot Spring Fountain Area): {{ウィキ座標度分秒|35|48|27.2|N|137|14|27.4|E|scale:20000}} === Foot Baths === Numerous foot baths are installed throughout Gero Onsen.{{Cite web|url=http://www.gero-spa.or.jp/ashiyu/|title=Foot Bath Guide|publisher={{ill|Gero Onsen Ryokan Cooperative|ja|下呂温泉旅館協同組合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen Ryokan Cooperative|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135493039}}|date=2018-09-03|accessdate=2019-10-13|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20180903005904/http://www.gero-spa.or.jp/ashiyu/|archivedate=2018-09-03}} The very first, known as “Sagi no Ashiyu” (Heron Foot Bath), was established in 2000 (Heisei 12).{{Cite web|url=https://www.gero-spa.com/shukuhaku/|title=New Attraction in Gero: "Sagi no Ashiyu"|publisher={{ill|Gero Onsen Ryokan Cooperative|ja|下呂温泉旅館協同組合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen Ryokan Cooperative|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135493039}}|date=2000-07-05|accessdate=2019-10-13|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20191013132437/http://www.gero-spa.or.jp/blog/page/44|archivedate=2019-10-13}} === Fire-Fishing === Fire-Fishing is a traditional method that exploits the natural behavior of {{ill|ayu|ja|鮎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ayu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アユ|1=Ayu sweetfish|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q642906}} (sweetfish) – which, when descending the river to spawn, are driven by setting nets and using bonfires and torches to force them into the nets. This technique is practiced on the {{ill|Maze River (Japan)|en|Maze River (Japan)|qid=Q258070|lt=Masase River|1=Maze River (Japan)}} annually from late August to late September.{{Cite web|url=https://www.gero-spa.com/spot/detail.php?id=132|title=Fire-Fishing on the Masase River|access-date=2022-10-25|publisher={{ill|Gero Onsen Tourism Association|ja|下呂温泉観光協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen Tourism Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}}} === Tourist Facilities and Landmarks === * {{ill|Gero Departure Onsen Museum|ja|下呂発 温泉博物館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Departure Onsen Museum|1=Gero Museum of Hot Springs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11360705}} * {{ill|Gero Onsen Gasshō Village|ja|下呂温泉合掌村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen Gasshō Village|1=Gero Onsen Gasshō-mura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11360703}} * {{ill|Onsen-dera|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen-dera|1=Onsen Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11562939}} (in Gero City) * {{ill|Morimizumu Hachiman Shrine|ja|森水無八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Morimizumu Hachiman Shrine|1=Mori Minashi Hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11539924}} * {{ill|Yunomachi Ujo Park|ja|湯のまち雨情公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunomachi Ujo Park|qq=}} == Souvenirs == * Okudaya Gero Ointment – A locally produced medicated plaster made with a blend of ingredients including {{ill|Ou-baku|ja|黄蘗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ou-baku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Kihada) and {{ill|Yamamo-mo|ja|楊梅皮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamo-mo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマモモ|1=Myrica rubra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q311617}} (Yōbaibi), manufactured by {{ill|Okudaya Matayemon Kōhonpo|ja|奥田又右衛門膏本舗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okudaya Matayemon Kōhonpo|1=Okudaya Matayemon Kōhonpo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11445999}}. * {{ill|Koya Leaf Miso|ja|朴葉味噌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koya Leaf Miso|1=hōba miso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11521610}} – Miso wrapped in cypress (kōya) leaves; some varieties include ground {{ill|Hida Beef|ja|飛騨牛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hida Beef|1=Hida Beef|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11665910}}. * Tochi-no-mi Senbei – Rice crackers made with {{ill|Tochi-no-mi|ja|栃の実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tochi-no-mi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トチノキ|1=Aesculus turbinata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1132580}}, known for their sweetness and soft texture.{{Cite book|和書|title=Okashi Fūdoki|date=1965th year 8月15日|year=1965|publisher={{ill|Hayakawa Publishing|en|Hayakawa Publishing|qid=Q11511323|lt=Hayakawa Shobō|1=Hayakawa Publishing}}|pages=110-111}} * {{ill|Sarubobo|en|Sarubobo|qid=Q1697067|lt=Sarubobo|1=Sarubobo}} – Literally “baby monkey,” a faceless doll dressed in red. * Pickled Items – A variety of pickles made from local Hida specialties such as “aka kabura” (red turnips) are widely available in souvenir shops and local supermarkets. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == {{Wiki Portal Link|Hot Springs|[[File:Hot springs 001.svg|34px|Portal: Hot Springs]]}} * {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|qid=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}}, {{ill|Spa town|en|Spa town|qid=Q4946461|lt=Onsen town|1=Spa town}}, {{ill|Public Bath|ja|外湯|1=Sotoyu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Public Bath|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}, {{ill|Hot Spring Rankings|ja|温泉番付|1=onsen banzuke|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Rankings|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Rankings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11562960}} * {{ill|List of hot springs in Japan|en|List of hot springs in Japan|qid=Q11086467|lt=List of hot springs in Japan|1=List of hot springs in Japan}}, {{ill|Japan's Three Famous Hot Springs|ja|日本三名泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan's Three Famous Hot Springs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三大一覧|1=Japan's Top 3|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10311536}} * {{ill|Gifu Prefectural Gero Onsen Hospital|ja|岐阜県立下呂温泉病院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gifu Prefectural Gero Onsen Hospital|1=Gifu Prefectural Gero Hospital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11471474}} * {{ill|Mayumi Mayu|ja|麻友美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mayumi Mayu|1=Tomomi Asa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11677455}} – Gero Onsen hometown {{ill|Tourism Ambassador|ja|観光大使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tourism Ambassador|1=tourism ambassador|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11630514}}. == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Gero Onsen}} * [http://www.gero-spa.com/ {{bad link|These are causing so much issues|Gero Onsen Tourism Association|ja|下呂温泉観光協会}]] *
